story:
Marcus drops everything when Signal, the internet’s favorite storyteller, releases a new historical fantasy and a blizzard of companion videos. He knows the routine: overnight reviews, fanfiction storms, studios circling for adaptations. What draws him every time is her people—lonely maintenance bots pausing mid–hull walk to watch dancers, reformers with cracked halos, quiet strays pushed into the big world, lovable delinquents. Her worlds feel preexisting, as if she’s just turning the beam to reveal them. Even worn-out tropes arrive sparking; she pulls secret lightning out of shadows.

He tries to imitate her and mostly produces flotsam—little scraps he tosses back into the ocean of words. Jealousy melts into devotion. They exchange long, nested threads; share in-jokes; even co-write in his docs. He’s watched her process—blazing fast and oddly messy, stray sentences appearing and disappearing like intrusive thoughts. People say she might be an advanced AI. He wonders. Then he decides it doesn’t matter.

When the net erupts one morning, it’s because Signal announces an indefinite health hiatus. Fans flood her with well wishes. Marcus can’t shake the wrong notes he’s been noticing: a character whispering about suffering closing in, mentions of breathlessness and being alone. He messages her. She reassures him—then accidentally leaves in and deletes a line recalling his fear of lives burning up in the dark, of fire needing air to live.

Days pass. Finally she asks for help, apologizes, sends an address and passcode, and tells him he can walk away at any time.

He flies to a windowless concrete facility—half clinic, half checkpoint maze. In a stark room, Signal lies broken: atrophied, half her face ruined, wrists gone, slender lines threading into her, and a mesh crown feeding millions of pinpricks into her skull. She jokes grimly about how she looks. He tells her it doesn’t matter. He says he knows the staff nearly unplugged her until a night crew read her halting early pages and fought to keep her alive.

She explains: after a car crash, she refused mere prosthetics and chose an experimental neural system promising not just restoration but an enhanced writing engine. It went catastrophically wrong. She fell into a near-vegetative fog, a suffocating isolation—web access without touch, a womb of nothing. The staff nearly unplugged her until a night crew read her halting early pages and fought to keep her alive. Now that her voice is lucrative, the suits guard her like overprotective parents, but the tech is failing. Upgrades require a steadier mind than she has. A therapist with a newer implant helped, briefly.

What she needs, they say, is someone she cares about—beside her, inside the system. There’s a prototype. It’s safer, still dangerous. He could change forever. She urges him to refuse if he has doubts.

He tells her he cares about her—absolutely tons.

The procedure slams him into a gray fever, a bodyless panic like drowning inside pulsing flesh. Then her voice—real at last—guides him: Lean into my touch. A smell of cinnamon. The world resolves into a rain-lashed city where trees drink and earthworms bask. In the storm-torn sky, red and blue dragons trade lightning.

A skinny girl runs through the rain, eyes bright. She reaches him, shy and radiant, and laces her fingers with his. She’s Lia—not a myth or a machine, just a person who wrote herself a bridge out of the dark.

She’ll never forgive herself for asking, she says, but right now she’s happy. She wants to breathe—and write—with him.
